refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of: (1) the measurement period and baseline, (2) what constitutes 'available' vs. 'unavailable' (e.g., does safety shutdown count as unavailability?), (3) the scope of the system (vehicle only, or full NGHE solution including teleoperation and support services?), and (4) the role of planned vs. unplanned downtime. High safety criticality demands that safety-driven shutdowns are tracked separately from availability metrics to avoid perverse incentives.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish a telemetry-based availability tracking system that logs state transitions (operational, maintenance, fault, safety-shutdown) with timestamps. Calculate availability as (total_time - unplanned_downtime - safety_shutdown_time) / total_time. Conduct acceptance testing over the agreed evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days) with independent audit of logs.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all unplanned downtime events (faults, failures, degradation) with root cause and duration.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall implement predictive and preventive maintenance scheduling to minimize unplanned downtime.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to the Site Manager and Maintenance team.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall isolate and recover from transient faults without requiring manual intervention or extended downtime.
FR-NGHE-004.5: Safety Services shall not degrade availability measurement; safety-driven shutdowns shall be logged separately and excluded from availability KPI calculation.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant subsystems (dual motors, dual control channels, dual teleoperation links) with automatic failover to eliminate single points of failure.
DP-B: Implement comprehensive health monitoring, predictive diagnostics, and condition-based maintenance scheduling to detect and prevent failures before they cause downtime.
DP-C: Implement modular architecture with hot-swappable components (battery packs, hydraulic modules, control units) to enable rapid field replacement and minimize repair time.

DP-D: Implement a combination of redundancy (DP-A), health monitoring (DP-B), and modularity (DP-C) with a centralized availability management system that orchestrates maintenance, failover, and recovery.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a multi-layered availability strategy combining: (1) selective redundancy for critical safety-related subsystems (dual control channels, dual teleoperation links), (2) comprehensive health monitoring and predictive diagnostics across all major subsystems (powertrain, hydraulics, electrical, teleoperation), (3) modular component design enabling rapid field replacement, and (4) a centralized Availability Management System (AMS) that integrates telemetry, maintenance scheduling, fault isolation, and failover orchestration to achieve ≥0.97 availability.

potential_sources: Health monitoring (DP-B) must couple with Vehicle telemetry interface to collect sensor data; this is a natural, low-coupling dependency.
Failover logic (DP-A) must couple with Safety Services to ensure safety-critical decisions are not overridden by availability optimization; this requires explicit safety gate logic.
Maintenance scheduling (DP-B) must couple with Site Manager tasking interface to coordinate downtime windows; this is a manageable coordination dependency.
Modular replacement (DP-C) couples with Vehicle design but is a design-time decision, not a runtime coupling.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is a composite strategy that integrates multiple design approaches. The couplings are well-understood and manageable through explicit interface contracts (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking). No hidden or problematic couplings identified.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Redundancy for safety-critical subsystems (dual control channels, dual teleoperation links) is technically and economically feasible within the NGHE design envelope.
ASSUMPTION: Predictive diagnostics algorithms can be developed and validated to reliably detect incipient failures with low false-positive rates.
ASSUMPTION: Field replacement of modular components can be performed by trained maintenance personnel within acceptable time windows (e.g., <2 hours).
RISK: Over-reliance on redundancy may mask underlying design or manufacturing quality issues; health monitoring must be paired with root cause analysis and continuous improvement.
RISK: Predictive diagnostics may require machine learning models trained on historical failure data; if such data is unavailable, the strategy may degrade to reactive maintenance.
RISK: Modular design may increase system complexity, weight, or cost; trade-off analysis required.
RISK: Availability target of 0.97 may be unachievable if the underlying failure rate of components is too high; component selection and supplier quality are critical.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) which subsystems require redundancy (recommend: control channels, teleoperation links, critical sensors), (2) the target mean time between failures (MTBF) and mean time to repair (MTTR) for each subsystem to support the 0.97 availability target, (3) the predictive diagnostics approach (e.g., condition-based thresholds, machine learning, physics-based models), and (4) the modular component list and field replacement procedures. Conduct a Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) to validate that the combined strategy addresses all critical failure modes.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. The Availability Management System (AMS) is a cross-cutting concern that resides at the NGHE Solution level and orchestrates health monitoring, maintenance scheduling, failover, and recovery across all subsystems. The AMS consumes Telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation, consumes SafetyAlert from Safety Services, and produces Tasking commands to coordinate maintenance and operational decisions.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Health monitoring and diagnostics data from Vehicle (powertrain, hydraulics, electrical, structural sensors) and Teleoperation (link quality, latency, command success rate). Used by AMS to detect faults, predict failures, and assess system state.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety-critical alerts from Safety Services (e.g., emergency stop, safety shutdown, hazard detection). AMS must respect safety gates and not override safety decisions with availability optimization.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Maintenance scheduling, failover commands, and operational directives from AMS to Vehicle and Teleoperation. Coordinates downtime windows, component replacement, and recovery actions.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communication backbone for Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Tasking. AMS depends on reliable network connectivity; network failures directly impact availability. Redundant network paths recommended for critical teleoperation links.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level boundary for the Availability Management System. The AMS is a cross-cutting orchestration layer that integrates health monitoring, maintenance, and failover across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Containment is high because the AMS logic, state machines, and decision algorithms are centralized at the NGHE Solution level. Diffusion is minimal (5%) because some health monitoring sensors reside in Vehicle and Teleoperation, but the aggregation and decision logic is centralized. This is the correct architectural choice.

Vehicle could host local health monitoring and fault detection (e.g., onboard diagnostics, local failover logic for redundant subsystems). However, the global availability orchestration, maintenance scheduling, and cross-system failover decisions must reside at the NGHE Solution level. Containment score reflects that Vehicle hosts subsystem-level health monitoring but not system-level availability management. Diffusion risk is high if availability logic is scattered across Vehicle and NGHE Solution.

Teleoperation could host health monitoring for teleoperation link quality and redundancy failover (e.g., switching between primary and backup communication channels). However, the integration with Vehicle health, maintenance scheduling, and safety gates must be at the NGHE Solution level. Containment score reflects that Teleoperation hosts link-level health monitoring but not system-level availability management. Diffusion risk is high if availability logic is split between Teleoperation and NGHE Solution.

Safety Services is responsible for safety-critical decisions (emergency stop, hazard detection, safety shutdown). The Availability Management System must respect safety gates and not override safety decisions. Safety Services should emit SafetyAlert signals to AMS, but the availability orchestration logic must not reside in Safety Services. Containment score is low because Safety Services has a different concern (safety) than availability. Diffusion risk is very high if availability logic leaks into Safety Services, creating potential conflicts between safety and availability optimization.

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: NGHE Solution (System Engineering team or Availability Management subsystem owner)
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Availability Management System (AMS) should be a dedicated subsystem or architectural layer within the NGHE Solution that owns the availability KPI. The AMS integrates health monitoring data from Vehicle and Teleoperation, respects safety gates from Safety Services, and orchestrates maintenance, failover, and recovery decisions. This centralized approach minimizes diffusion and ensures that availability logic is not scattered across multiple modules.
containment_risks: RISK: If health monitoring logic is duplicated across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution, the system will suffer from inconsistent fault detection and conflicting recovery decisions. Mitigation: Define a clear contract for health monitoring data (Telemetry interface) and centralize decision logic in AMS.

RISK: If maintenance scheduling is owned by multiple teams (Vehicle maintenance, Teleoperation maintenance, Site Manager), coordination failures may lead to missed maintenance windows or conflicting downtime schedules. Mitigation: Centralize maintenance scheduling in AMS and expose a Tasking interface for Site Manager coordination.

RISK: If failover logic is distributed across Vehicle (local redundancy) and NGHE Solution (global orchestration), there may be race conditions or conflicting failover decisions. Mitigation: Define clear failover policies and ensure that local failover (e.g., dual motor switching) is coordinated with global AMS decisions.

RISK: If Safety Services and AMS have conflicting priorities (safety vs. availability), the system may enter unsafe states or fail to meet availability targets. Mitigation: Establish explicit safety gates in AMS that prevent availability optimization from overriding safety decisions. Safety alerts must always take precedence.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish a dedicated Availability Management System (AMS) subsystem within the NGHE Solution with clear ownership and interfaces. Define the AMS architecture as a state machine or event-driven system that consumes Telemetry and SafetyAlert, maintains a model of system health and availability, and produces Tasking commands. Specify the health monitoring data schema (Telemetry interface) to ensure consistent fault detection across Vehicle and Teleoperation. Define failover policies and safety gates to prevent conflicts between availability optimization and safety requirements. Allocate specific sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.5) to AMS, Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services to clarify ownership boundaries.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR (achieve ≥0.97 availability over the agreed evaluation period) is clearly mapped to DP-D (multi-layered availability strategy combining redundancy, health monitoring, modularity, and centralized orchestration). The DP directly addresses the FR by reducing unplanned downtime through fault prevention, rapid detection, and quick recovery. The DP is feasible and aligns with industry best practices for high-availability systems. No decoupling issues identified.

diffusion_risks: Health monitoring logic may be distributed across Vehicle (local sensors), Teleoperation (link quality), and NGHE Solution (AMS aggregation). Mitigation: Define a clear Telemetry interface contract and centralize decision logic in AMS.

Failover logic may be split between Vehicle (local redundancy switching) and NGHE Solution (global orchestration). Mitigation: Define failover policies and ensure coordination through Tasking interface.

Maintenance scheduling may involve Site Manager (Tasking input), Vehicle (component replacement), and AMS (scheduling logic). Mitigation: Centralize scheduling in AMS and expose Tasking interface for Site Manager coordination.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The Availability Management System is appropriately assigned to the NGHE Solution boundary, which is the correct level for cross-system orchestration. Vehicle and Teleoperation will host subsystem-level health monitoring, but the global availability decisions will be centralized in AMS. Safety Services will emit SafetyAlert signals but will not own availability logic. This containment strategy minimizes diffusion and ensures clear ownership.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: The refined FR is measurable and verifiable through telemetry-based availability tracking. The AMS can log state transitions and calculate availability metrics. Acceptance testing can be conducted over the agreed evaluation period with independent audit of logs. Verification is feasible and aligns with the Telemetry interface.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The FR is allocated to the NGHE Solution (AMS) with sub-FRs allocated to Vehicle (health monitoring, fault detection), Teleoperation (link health, redundancy), Safety Services (safety gates), and Site Manager (maintenance coordination). Allocation is clear and ownership boundaries are well-defined.

recommended_revisions: Refine the DP to specify target MTBF and MTTR for each subsystem, ensuring that the combined strategy achieves the 0.97 availability target. Conduct a quantitative availability analysis (e.g., Markov models, fault tree analysis) to validate the target.
Define the health monitoring data schema (Telemetry interface) in detail, including sensor types, sampling rates, data formats, and fault detection thresholds.
Establish explicit safety gates in the AMS to prevent availability optimization from overriding safety decisions. Document the priority rules and conflict resolution mechanisms.

Define the failover policies and procedures for each critical subsystem (e.g., dual motor switching, teleoperation link failover, control channel redundancy). Specify the failover latency and success criteria.
Allocate sub-FRs to specific modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, AMS) and establish clear ownership and interface contracts.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-004 is a high-level KPI that requires decomposition into lower-level functional requirements and design parameters. The refined FR and selected DP are sound, but the implementation requires detailed design of the Availability Management System, health monitoring architecture, failover logic, and maintenance scheduling. The sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.5) must be decomposed further to define specific design parameters for each subsystem. Additionally, the high safety criticality of the system demands that safety gates and conflict resolution mechanisms be explicitly designed and validated. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to ensure that the availability strategy is fully realized and integrated with safety requirements.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all unplanned downtime events (faults, failures, degradation) with root cause and duration.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall implement predictive and preventive maintenance scheduling to minimize unplanned downtime.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to the Site Manager and Maintenance team.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall isolate and recover from transient faults without requiring manual intervention or extended downtime.
FR-NGHE-004.5: Safety Services shall not degrade availability measurement; safety-driven shutdowns shall be logged separately and excluded from availability KPI calculation.

assumptions: Redundancy for safety-critical subsystems (dual control channels, dual teleoperation links) is technically and economically feasible.
Predictive diagnostics algorithms can be developed and validated to reliably detect incipient failures with low false-positive rates.
Field replacement of modular components can be performed by trained maintenance personnel within acceptable time windows (e.g., <2 hours).
The customer-defined availability target of 0.97 is achievable with the proposed multi-layered strategy.
Safety Services and Availability Management System can be designed with explicit safety gates to prevent conflicts between safety and availability optimization.
Telemetry interface can be standardized across Vehicle and Teleoperation to enable consistent health monitoring and fault detection.
open_risks: RISK: Over-reliance on redundancy may mask underlying design or manufacturing quality issues. Mitigation: Pair redundancy with root cause analysis and continuous improvement.

RISK: Predictive diagnostics may require machine learning models trained on historical failure data. If such data is unavailable, the strategy may degrade to reactive maintenance. Mitigation: Establish data collection and model training plan early in the project.
RISK: Modular design may increase system complexity, weight, or cost. Mitigation: Conduct trade-off analysis and validate that modularity benefits (rapid field replacement) justify the costs.

RISK: Availability target of 0.97 may be unachievable if the underlying failure rate of components is too high. Mitigation: Conduct component selection and supplier quality analysis to ensure MTBF targets are met.

RISK: Failover logic may have race conditions or conflicting decisions if distributed across Vehicle and NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Define clear failover policies and ensure coordination through Tasking interface.
RISK: Safety Services and AMS may have conflicting priorities. Mitigation: Establish explicit safety gates and priority rules to ensure safety always takes precedence.
evidence_needed: Quantitative availability analysis (Markov models, fault tree analysis) validating that the proposed strategy achieves 0.97 availability target.
Component MTBF and MTTR data from suppliers or industry standards to support the availability calculation.
Health monitoring data schema and fault detection algorithm specifications.
Failover policy and procedure documentation for each critical subsystem.
Safety gate specifications and conflict resolution mechanisms between Safety Services and AMS.
Maintenance scheduling algorithm and field replacement procedure documentation.
Telemetry interface contract and data format specifications.
Acceptance test plan and evaluation period definition (e.g., 30 days, 90 days).
